summary.intent: Wire the dead `resolve_effective_policy` scaffolding into the live runtime path and extend the config schema so per-channel, per-agent, and per-sender tool gating actually works for mixed-trust deployments.
summary.outcome: Fully implemented layered tool policy: typed schema additions (provider/channel/group/sender policy fields), `sender_id` propagated through all 6 channel handlers, `resolve_effective_policy` rewritten to use typed config and wired into `apply_runtime_tool_filters`, validation updated, 709 tests passing, zero clippy warnings, committed.

repo: `resolve_effective_policy` in `crates/tools/src/policy.rs` was dead code â€” called only by its own unit tests; the live runtime used a separate `effective_tool_policy()` that read only global config.
`AgentPreset` already had a `tools: PresetToolPolicy` field, so layer 3 (per-agent policy) required no new schema type, only wiring.

Channel configs used `HashMap<String, serde_json::Value>` with no typed substructure â€” `groups`, `tools.policy`, and `bySender` blocks were schema-drifted JSON pointer paths that could never resolve.

`PromptHostRuntimeContext` already carried `channel_type`, `channel_account_id`, `channel_chat_id`, and `channel_chat_type` â€” sufficient context to build `PolicyContext` at call sites without new plumbing.
`peer_id` (the sending user's platform ID) was available in every channel handler but was not included in `ChannelMessageMeta`, requiring a `sender_id` field addition to that struct.

The CUDA/llama-cpp crates fail to build on macOS (no CUDA toolkit) â€” this is a pre-existing environment issue unrelated to any code changes; scoped `cargo clippy` to changed crates to work around it.

There were 4 `apply_runtime_tool_filters` call sites in `crates/chat/src/lib.rs`: `list_tools` schema path (~5204), non-streaming send (~5415), streaming `full_context` (~5534), and `run_with_tools` (~6663), each with slightly different available variables.

code.finding: Entire file rewritten to use typed `MoltisConfig` instead of raw JSON pointer traversal; `moltis-config` was already a declared dependency so no Cargo.toml change needed.
Live runtime used `effective_tool_policy()` (global-only) here; replaced with `resolve_effective_policy` accepting a `PolicyContext` built from runtime context and params.
`apply_runtime_tool_filters` call in `list_tools` schema path â€” needs PolicyContext but has no channel/sender context, so a minimal context with only global+provider layers is built.

Added `policy: Option<ToolPolicyConfig>` to `ProviderEntry`; added new types `ChannelToolPolicyOverride`, `GroupToolPolicy` with `allow`/`deny`/`by_sender` fields; added `tools: Option<ChannelToolPolicyOverride>` to channel account map entries.
Added `sender_id: Option<String>` to `ChannelMessageMeta` struct â€” required updating all 6 channel handler construction sites.
Updated `build_schema_map()` to recognize `policy` under provider entries and `tools` under channel account configs; without this, valid config keys would fail schema validation.
New public types `ChannelToolPolicyOverride` and `GroupToolPolicy` added to crate re-exports so downstream crates can use them without direct schema module imports.

workflow: Running `cargo clippy -p <crate>` scoped to changed crates is an effective workaround when environment-specific build failures (CUDA, CMake) block workspace-wide lint runs.
Using a beads issue per logical sub-task (schema, sender_id threading, runtime wiring) with a dependency chain provides clean tracking for multi-crate changes landed in a single session.
Reading all affected files in parallel before making edits significantly reduces round-trips when a change fans out across many files (6 channel handlers + core config + runtime).
Grepping for all struct construction sites (`ChannelMessageMeta {`) before adding a field catches all update locations that would otherwise cause compile errors.

friction: CUDA/llama-cpp crates fail to build on macOS, blocking workspace-wide `cargo clippy` and `just lint` â€” required scoping lint to individual crates.

Four `apply_runtime_tool_filters` call sites in `chat/src/lib.rs` each had different variable availability (`params` present or absent, different `agent_id` binding names), requiring separate reads of surrounding context for each.
Line numbers shift after each edit to a large file (`lib.rs`), requiring re-grepping call sites between edits to avoid applying hunks at stale offsets.
The unused `profile_tools` variable warning only surfaced after compilation, not during the edit phase â€” a small but avoidable cleanup step.
open_items: The `by_sender` deny/allow list in `GroupToolPolicy` enables per-sender gating but there is no UI or docs for channel admins to configure it â€” needs documentation and possibly a config example.

Hook payloads (#640) were noted as needing `channel_metadata` for the alternative shell-hook approach; that issue may still be open and could benefit from the now-available `sender_id` in `ChannelMessageMeta`.
The `list_tools` schema path builds a minimal `PolicyContext` with no channel/sender context â€” this means tool listings shown to users won't reflect per-channel restrictions, a UX gap to revisit.

No integration test covers the full layered merge (global â†’ provider â†’ agent â†’ channel â†’ group â†’ sender â†’ sandbox) end-to-end; only unit tests on the policy module itself were confirmed passing.
A response to GitHub discussion #641 was proposed but not drafted or posted â€” the author's use case is now implemented but they haven't been notified.
